name: Stroudsbrug
short_description: Historic rockhounding area in the Delaware Valley offering access to quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens from Precambrian metamorphic bedrock. Flat terrain near Smithfield Township.
description:
Stroudsbrug is a well-known rockhounding destination in Monroe County, Pennsylvania, situated within the geologically rich Delaware Valley region. The site provides access to specimens from the Precambrian metamorphic basement rocks that underlie this portion of the Pocono Plateau. Collectors here commonly find quartz, feldspar, and mica in various forms, with opportunities for both field specimen collection and study of local mineral assemblages.

The area's geology reflects the ancient collision of continental plates during the formation of the Appalachian Mountains. The metamorphic rocks exposed here contain the mineralogy typical of medium-grade metamorphism, with quartz veins and feldspathic pegmatites providing excellent collecting opportunities. The relatively flat terrain at 298 feet elevation makes this an accessible site for collectors of varying experience levels.

The landscape surrounding Stroudsbrug consists of mixed woodland with accessible outcrops and cleared areas suitable for rockhounding. Many collectors focus on hillside exposures and weathered material along stream banks where specimens weather naturally from the host rock. The accessibility of the site and diversity of mineral specimens make it a popular destination for both individual collectors and organized rockhounding club trips.

Best results come from examining fresh exposures, working along natural erosion lines, and searching in areas where weathering has loosened specimens from the bedrock matrix. The combination of Precambrian bedrock geology and accessible public or private land with a long rockhounding tradition makes Stroudsbrug a valuable collecting location for Pennsylvania mineral enthusiasts.
history: Stroudsbrug sits within the Pocono Plateau region, where Precambrian metamorphic rocks were thrust upward and exposed during the Appalachian orogeny approximately 300 million years ago. The quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens found here formed under medium-grade metamorphic conditions deep within the ancient mountain chain, and were brought to near-surface levels through tectonic uplift and subsequent erosion of overlying rock layers.
tips: Search weathered hillside exposures and along natural erosion lines where specimens have already loosened from the host rock, making collection easier and more productive. Bring a chisel and small pry bar to carefully extract quartz and feldspar specimens from pegmatite veins running through the metamorphic basement rock. Look for translucent to milky quartz, cream to pink feldspar, and silvery mica flakes in areas where recent erosion has exposed fresh material. Early spring after winter freeze-thaw cycles often yields the best specimens as natural weathering processes expose new material.
terrain_notes: Flat terrain at 298 feet elevation in a mixed deciduous woodland setting. Walking distances are generally short from parking areas to productive collecting zones. Footing is uneven with scattered rocks and root systems typical of established forest, but no significant elevation gain required. Recent clearing or maintenance areas may provide better access to outcrop material than heavily wooded sections.
best_season: April - October. Spring through early fall offers the most comfortable collecting conditions in this region. Avoid winter months when frozen ground and snow cover make digging difficult, and late fall through early spring when wet conditions create muddy footing in the woodland terrain.
written_directions: From Smithfield Township center, take PA-191 north approximately 2 miles. Look for documented rockhounding access points or private property with known collecting permissions. GPS coordinates 40.990457, -75.134704 mark the general area. Note: Verify current access status and obtain any necessary permissions before visiting, as some portions may be private land.
road_conditions: Paved roads to general vicinity. Local road conditions typically passable year-round, though forest service roads or property access roads may become muddy during wet seasons.
parking_notes: Limited informal parking near collection areas. No developed parking lot. Verify parking permissions with property owner or local rockhounding club.
collection_rules: Verify access permissions with property owner before collecting. Respect all posted property boundaries and no-trespassing signs. Remove only specimens small enough to carry by hand. Do not use motorized equipment. Leave collecting sites in good condition for future rockhounders.
rules:
Obtain property owner permission before collecting
Respect all posted signs and property boundaries
Remove only hand-portable specimens
No motorized equipment
Leave sites clean and undamaged
nearest_services: Smithfield Township center approximately 2 miles south, with gas, food, and water services available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with minimal elevation change, accessible to most hikers with basic mobility. Uneven forest floor with rocks and roots requires sturdy footwear but no scrambling or climbing needed. No wheelchair access due to natural woodland terrain and lack of maintained paths.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can you find rockhounding at Stroudsbrug, Pennsylvania?
faq.answer: The primary finds at Stroudsbrug are quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Quartz appears in translucent to milky varieties, feldspar ranges from cream to pink, and mica presents as silvery flakes. These minerals formed in Precambrian metamorphic rocks and are exposed through natural erosion and weathering processes in this portion of the Delaware Valley.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Stroudsbrug, and what are the access requirements?
faq.answer: Stroudsbrug is on private land, so you must verify current access permissions with the property owner before collecting. There are no established fees, but trespassing without permission is prohibited. Contact local rockhounding clubs or the Monroe County area for information about accessing this historic collecting site.

faq.question: When is the best time to go rockhounding at Stroudsbrug near Smithfield Township?
faq.answer: The best collecting season is April through October when weather is pleasant and ground conditions are optimal. Spring months are particularly productive as freeze-thaw cycles from winter naturally loosen specimens from the host rock. Avoid winter when frozen ground and snow make collecting difficult.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Stroudsbrug?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer or chisel and small pry bar for carefully extracting quartz and feldspar from pegmatite veins. A collecting bag, gloves for protection from sharp materials, and sturdy hiking boots for the woodland terrain are essential. Hand lenses help identify mineral specimens and assess quality before extraction.

faq.question: Is Stroudsbrug rockhounding site suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Stroudsbrug is beginner-friendly with easy, flat terrain and minimal hiking required to productive areas. Children ages 6 and up can participate with supervision. The accessible location, diverse mineral types, and lack of extreme hazards make it an excellent introduction to rockhounding in Pennsylvania.

faq.question: Where exactly should I search for the best specimens at Stroudsbrug?
faq.answer: Focus your search on weathered hillside exposures and areas showing natural erosion lines where specimens have loosened from the matrix. Stream banks and cleared areas where bedrock is exposed tend to be most productive. Look for pegmatite veins running through the metamorphic rock, as these contain the highest concentration of collectible quartz and feldspar specimens.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Stroudsbrug near East Stroudsburg, PA. Easy accessible rockhounding in Precambrian metamorphic bedrock. Verify access first.
